# ARTICLE 1 (Dev.to)

**Title:** i built 258 websites in 35 days with zero backend. here's the stack.

**Tags:** `webdev` `pwa` `javascript` `opensource`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** terminal window showing multiple surge deploy commands, 258 sites live at $0 hosting cost

---

258 websites. 35 days. $0 hosting. no backend. no database. no server. every single one is live right now and loads in under 2 seconds.

i'm not exaggerating the number. 28 marketing pages, 14 streak tracking apps, 7 PWA tools, 6 comparison sites, 10 lead magnet calculators, and about 190 local business demo sites. all running on the same stack.

here's exactly how.

## the stack

every site is a single HTML file. one file. that's the entire app.

```
project/
  index.html    ← the whole app
  manifest.json ← PWA metadata
  sw.js         ← service worker for offline
  200.html      ← SPA fallback (copy of index.html)
```

no React. no Next.js. no Vite. no Webpack. no build step. no node_modules folder eating 400MB of disk space.

the HTML file contains everything. styles in a `<style>` tag. logic in a `<script>` tag. layout in the markup. one file, one deploy, one URL.

## why single-file architecture works

i tried the framework route first. spent 3 days setting up a Next.js project for one app. by the time I had the build pipeline, CI/CD, and hosting sorted out, i could have shipped 15 single-file sites.

the math:

- framework setup per project: 2-4 hours (dependencies, config, build, deploy pipeline)
- single-file setup per project: 8 minutes (write HTML, `surge .`, done)

at 258 sites, that's 516-1,032 hours saved. not a typo.

## offline-first with localStorage

every app stores data in `localStorage`. sleep logs, streak counts, email drafts, calculator inputs. zero API calls. zero auth. zero database bills.

```javascript
// save data
const saveData = (key, value) => {
  localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(value));
};

// load data
const loadData = (key, fallback = null) => {
  const raw = localStorage.getItem(key);
  return raw ? JSON.parse(raw) : fallback;
};
```

the service worker caches the HTML file on first visit. after that, the app works with no internet connection.

```javascript
// sw.js - the entire service worker
const CACHE = 'v1';
const ASSETS = ['/', '/index.html', '/manifest.json'];

self.addEventListener('install', e => {
  e.waitUntil(caches.open(CACHE).then(c => c.addAll(ASSETS)));
});

self.addEventListener('fetch', e => {
  e.respondWith(
    caches.match(e.request).then(r => r || fetch(e.request))
  );
});
```

that's it. 10 lines. the app now works offline forever.

## deployment: one command, every time

```bash
surge . myapp-name.surge.sh
```

surge.sh is free static hosting. no dashboard. no billing page. no config files. you run that command and the site is live in under 30 seconds.

i used the same command 258 times. never once hit a rate limit or site cap.

the 200.html file handles SPA routing. it's a copy of index.html. surge serves it for any route that doesn't match a static file. single-page app routing without a router library.

## what i actually built with this

a few live examples you can try right now:

- [pagescorer-vs-gtmetrix.surge.sh](https://pagescorer-vs-gtmetrix.surge.sh) - free website speed test, no signup
- [coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh](https://coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh) - cold email tool comparison
- [sleepmaxx-vs-sleepcycle.surge.sh](https://sleepmaxx-vs-sleepcycle.surge.sh) - sleep tracker comparison
- [focuslock-web.surge.sh](https://focuslock-web.surge.sh) - pomodoro timer PWA
- [sleepmaxx-web.surge.sh](https://sleepmaxx-web.surge.sh) - sleep tracking PWA

every one of those is a single HTML file with localStorage persistence and offline support. open them on your phone. add to home screen. they behave like native apps.

## PWA manifest in 30 seconds

```json
{
  "name": "My App",
  "short_name": "App",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#09090b",
  "theme_color": "#09090b",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "data:image/svg+xml,...",
      "sizes": "any",
      "type": "image/svg+xml"
    }
  ]
}
```

inline SVG icons. no image files to host. the manifest points to a data URI. one less asset to manage across 258 sites.

## the pattern i repeat every time

1. write index.html (CSS + JS inline, dark theme, responsive)
2. add manifest.json (app name, icon, theme color)
3. add sw.js (10-line service worker)
4. copy index.html to 200.html
5. run `surge . app-name.surge.sh`

total time: 8-45 minutes depending on complexity. the streak apps took about 15 minutes each once i had the pattern down. the comparison sites took 30-45 minutes because the content is longer.

## what this approach is bad for

let's be honest about the tradeoffs.

- no server-side rendering. bad for content that needs SEO with dynamic data.
- no database. if you need multi-user data or accounts, this won't work.
- no backend API. everything is client-side. you can't process payments or send emails from the app itself.
- localStorage has a 5-10MB limit per origin. fine for text data, bad for images.

if you need any of those things, use a real framework. this stack is for tools, calculators, trackers, landing pages, comparison sites, and single-player apps.

## the numbers

- 258 sites deployed
- $0/month hosting cost
- average deploy time: 22 seconds
- average page weight: 45KB (gzipped)
- average load time: 0.8 seconds
- 0 npm packages installed
- 0 build steps
- 0 CI/CD pipelines

if you're building anything that doesn't need a backend, try shipping a single HTML file first. you might not go back.

# ARTICLE 2 (Dev.to)

**Title:** free tools i built instead of paying for SaaS

**Tags:** `saas` `tools` `opensource` `webdev`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** comparison table showing SaaS tools with monthly prices crossed out, replaced with $0

---

i was paying $160/month across 5 different SaaS tools. now i pay $0 for the same functionality. i built the replacements myself.

not as weekend hobby projects that i abandoned after a week. these are live, deployed, and i use them daily. they run in the browser with zero backend. you can try all of them right now.

here's what i replaced, how much i save, and how each one works.

## 1. ColdMaxx vs Instantly ($30/mo saved)

**what i was paying:** Instantly.ai, $30/month for the Growth plan. email sequences, contact management, open tracking.

**what i built:** ColdMaxx. same core features. runs entirely in your browser. $0.

ColdMaxx handles email sequence drafting, follow-up scheduling, contact management, and template storage. all data lives in localStorage. no account, no login, no credit card.

what it doesn't do: automated warmup and inbox rotation across dozens of accounts. if you're sending 50,000+ emails a month across 30 inboxes, you need Instantly's infrastructure. if you're a solopreneur sending from 1-3 accounts, ColdMaxx covers it.

**full comparison:** [coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh](https://coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh)

**annual savings: $360**

## 2. PageScorer vs GTmetrix (free vs freemium)

**what i was dealing with:** GTmetrix requires account signup for advanced features. their paid plans start at $14.95/month. and every test makes you wait in a queue.

**what i built:** PageScorer. paste a URL, get a speed score instantly. no signup. no queue. no account. no email.

it checks load time, interactivity, visual stability. the stuff that actually affects whether people bounce from your site. no 47-page Lighthouse report that nobody reads.

**full comparison:** [pagescorer-vs-gtmetrix.surge.sh](https://pagescorer-vs-gtmetrix.surge.sh)

**annual savings: $179** (vs GTmetrix Solo plan)

## 3. SleepMaxx vs Sleep Cycle ($40/yr saved)

**what i was paying:** Sleep Cycle premium, $39.99/year. sleep tracking, smart alarms, sleep quality scores.

**what i built:** SleepMaxx (branded as Dusk). sleep logging, quality scoring, trend analysis, smart alarm suggestions. runs offline. keeps your data on your device. doesn't sell your sleep patterns to advertisers.

sleep cycle sends your data to their servers. they know when you go to bed, when you wake up, and how restless you are. SleepMaxx stores everything in localStorage on your phone. nothing leaves the device.

**try it:** [sleepmaxx-web.surge.sh](https://sleepmaxx-web.surge.sh)

**full comparison:** [sleepmaxx-vs-sleepcycle.surge.sh](https://sleepmaxx-vs-sleepcycle.surge.sh)

**annual savings: $40**

## 4. Revenue Leak Audit (self-hosted)

**the problem:** most SaaS audit tools charge $50-200/month to tell you you're overpaying for tools. that's ironic.

**what i built:** a free revenue leak audit calculator. you input your current tools and spending. it flags overlapping features, unused subscriptions, and cheaper alternatives. the output is a prioritized list of what to cut.

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/revenue-leak-audit](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

it runs in your browser. your financial data doesn't leave your machine. no "enter your email to see results" gate.

**potential savings: $200-2,000/yr** depending on your stack

## 5. SaaS Stack Audit

**the problem:** you sign up for tools, forget about them, and keep paying. everyone does this. the average solopreneur wastes $1,200/year on unused SaaS subscriptions according to Productiv's 2025 report.

**what i built:** a tool that walks you through every category of SaaS you might be paying for, checks if you need it, and suggests free alternatives. interactive, step by step, runs in your browser.

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/saas-stack-audit](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

**potential savings: $1,200/yr** (industry average for unused subscriptions)

## 6. Cold Email ROI Calculator

**the problem:** people run cold email campaigns without knowing their numbers. what's your cost per reply? cost per meeting? cost per close? if you can't answer those in 5 seconds, you're flying blind.

**what i built:** a calculator where you plug in your send volume, reply rate, meeting rate, and close rate. it shows you exact ROI, cost per acquisition, and whether your campaign is profitable.

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/cold-email-roi-calculator](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

## 7. Subject Line Grader

**the problem:** you write a cold email subject line and guess whether it's good. tools like Mailmeteor's grader are fine but limited.

**what i built:** a grader that scores your subject line on length, power words, personalization tokens, spam triggers, and readability. instant score with specific suggestions to improve.

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/subject-line-grader](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

## total savings

| tool replaced | monthly cost | annual cost | my alternative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instantly Growth | $30/mo | $360/yr | ColdMaxx |
| GTmetrix Solo | $14.95/mo | $179/yr | PageScorer |
| Sleep Cycle Premium | $3.33/mo | $40/yr | SleepMaxx |
| SaaS audit tools | ~$50/mo | ~$600/yr | Revenue Leak Audit |
| **total** | **~$98/mo** | **~$1,179/yr** | **$0** |

## how they're all built

same stack for every one:

- single HTML file (CSS + JS inline)
- localStorage for persistence
- service worker for offline
- surge.sh for free hosting

no backend. no database. no framework. no build step. total combined file size across all 7 tools: about 340KB.

## what i learned

most SaaS you pay for is a database, a UI, and some logic. if you only need the UI and the logic, and your data can stay local, you can build a free replacement in a few hours.

the hard part isn't the building. it's accepting that the free version won't have every feature. ColdMaxx doesn't have automated warmup. PageScorer doesn't test from 22 global locations. SleepMaxx doesn't have smart wake detection using your phone's accelerometer.

but for 80% of what i actually use these tools for, the free versions work fine.

$1,179/year back in my pocket. go build your own.

# ARTICLE 3 (Medium)

**Title:** how i use Claude Code to ship PWAs in hours, not weeks

**Tags:** AI, Programming, Productivity, Software Development, Claude

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Subtitle:** the vibe coding workflow that produced 28 streak apps, 19 demo sites, and 10 lead magnets in 5 weeks

---

five weeks ago i had zero shipped apps. today i have 28 streak tracking PWAs, 19 demo and marketing sites, 10 interactive lead magnet calculators, 6 comparison pages, and 7 standalone tool apps. all live. all deployed. all functional.

the tool that made this possible costs $20/month. it runs in my terminal. and it ships complete, working applications faster than any IDE i've ever used.

here's the workflow.

## what Claude Code actually is

if you've used Cursor, you know the AI IDE model. you write code in an editor, the AI suggests completions, you accept or reject them line by line.

Claude Code is different. it's a command-line agent. you tell it what to build, and it builds the entire thing. not line by line. the whole app. files, logic, styles, manifest, service worker. you review it, make corrections, and deploy.

the mental model shift: you stop being a typist and start being a director. you describe what you want, review what it produces, and iterate.

i wrote a full comparison here: [cursor-vs-claudecode.surge.sh](https://cursor-vs-claudecode.surge.sh)

## the workflow i actually use

### step 1: describe the app in plain english

i don't write specs. i don't draw wireframes. i open my terminal and describe what i want in conversational english.

a real prompt i used:

> build a sleep tracker PWA. single HTML file, dark theme, mobile-first. user logs sleep time and wake time. calculate total hours, show 7-day and 30-day averages. use localStorage. add a service worker for offline. make it installable.

that's it. that's the whole brief.

### step 2: Claude Code writes the complete app

it produces the HTML file with inline CSS and JavaScript, the manifest.json, the service worker, and the 200.html fallback. complete, working app in one pass.

the first version is usually 85-90% right. the CSS might need tweaking. a chart might render weird on small screens. the date math might be off by a timezone.

### step 3: iterate in the same session

i review the output and give corrections in plain english:

> the sleep duration calculation is wrong when bedtime is before midnight and wake time is after midnight. fix the cross-midnight logic. also make the chart bars wider on mobile.

Claude Code understands the existing code context. it modifies the right functions. i don't have to point it at specific lines or files. it knows where the bug is.

### step 4: deploy

```bash
surge . sleepmaxx-web.surge.sh
```

live in 22 seconds. the whole cycle from "i want an app" to "it's live on the internet" takes 15-45 minutes.

## why this is faster than the IDE approach

in Cursor or VS Code with Copilot, the workflow is:

1. create project structure (5 min)
2. install dependencies (3 min)
3. write boilerplate (10 min)
4. get AI autocomplete suggestions, accept/reject line by line (30 min)
5. debug build issues (15 min)
6. configure deployment (10 min)
7. deploy (5 min)

total: 78 minutes, and that's if nothing goes wrong.

with Claude Code:

1. describe what you want (2 min)
2. review output, give corrections (10 min)
3. deploy (1 min)

total: 13 minutes for a simple app. 30-45 minutes for something complex.

the difference isn't 2x. it's 3-6x. and it compounds. when you can ship 4 apps in the time it used to take to ship 1, you test ideas faster. you learn what works faster. you kill losers faster.

## what i actually shipped with this workflow

**28 streak tracking apps.** scripture streaks for 14 different faith traditions (catholic, orthodox, baptist, methodist, lutheran, episcopal, pentecostal, protestant, evangelical, anglican, presbyterian, sunni, shia, and a general scripture streak). plus 7 non-religious streak apps: fitness, coding, meditation, reading, art, journal, and language.

each one took about 15 minutes. same core architecture (streak counter, calendar view, stats, localStorage). Claude Code understood the pattern after the first 3 and got progressively faster.

**19 marketing and demo sites.** landing pages for each app, plus comparison pages and tool showcases. Claude Code handled the copy, the responsive layout, the structured data for SEO, and the dark theme CSS.

**10 lead magnet calculators.** cold email ROI calculator, subject line grader, revenue leak audit, SaaS stack audit, side project revenue estimator, solopreneur launch checklist, and more. each one is an interactive tool that runs in the browser.

**6 comparison sites.** ColdMaxx vs Instantly, PageScorer vs GTmetrix, SleepMaxx vs Sleep Cycle, Cursor vs Claude Code, Instantly vs Lemlist, ConvertKit vs Beehiiv. full SEO, FAQ schema, structured data.

## where Claude Code struggles

i won't pretend it's perfect.

**complex state management.** when an app has 8+ interdependent state variables, Claude Code sometimes loses track of the data flow. i had to manually debug the streak calculation logic on 3 of the 28 apps.

**pixel-perfect CSS on the first try.** the layouts are always functional but sometimes the spacing or font sizing feels slightly off. i usually spend 5-10 minutes tweaking CSS manually after Claude Code generates the initial version.

**multi-file architectures.** for these single-file PWAs, Claude Code is perfect. for a full React app with 40 components, shared state, and API routes, the context window starts to matter. you have to be more specific about which files to touch.

## the economics

$20/month for Claude Code (on the Max plan, which gives unlimited Opus usage).

$0/month for surge.sh hosting.

$0 for npm packages because there are no npm packages.

cost per app deployed: about $0.26 if you average the monthly subscription across 77 things shipped in 5 weeks.

compare that to hiring a developer at $50-150/hour. a simple PWA would cost $500-2,000 to commission. i shipped 77 for the cost of lunch.

## how to start

1. sign up for Claude Max ($20/month)
2. open your terminal
3. type `claude` to start a session
4. describe the simplest possible version of what you want
5. review, correct, deploy

don't start with your dream app. start with a calculator. a timer. a tracker. something you can ship in 30 minutes. get the muscle memory for the workflow. then scale up.

the gap between "i have an idea" and "it's live on the internet" used to be weeks. now it's minutes. that changes what's worth trying.

---

# ARTICLE 4 (Medium)

**Title:** the $0 cold email stack that replaces $100/mo tools

**Tags:** Email Marketing, SaaS, Startups, Cold Email, Business

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Subtitle:** i built free alternatives to Instantly, Lemlist, and every cold email SaaS charging monthly subscriptions

---

cold email tools are a racket.

Instantly charges $30/month. Lemlist charges $59/month. Smartlead charges $39/month. Saleshandy charges $25/month. stack up the "essentials" every cold email guru recommends and you're looking at $100-200/month before you've sent a single email.

i built a free stack that does 80% of what those tools do. it runs in your browser. no account required. no credit card. no "free trial that auto-charges you in 14 days."

here's every piece of it.

## the stack

| what it does | paid tool | price | my free version |
|---|---|---|---|
| email sequences + contact management | Instantly | $30/mo | ColdMaxx |
| ROI tracking | custom spreadsheets | $0 but 2 hours to set up | Cold Email ROI Calculator |
| subject line testing | various freemium tools | $0-15/mo | Subject Line Grader |
| tool comparison research | hours of googling | your time | comparison pages |

total monthly cost of the paid approach: $30-100+

total monthly cost of my approach: $0

## ColdMaxx: the free cold email tool

ColdMaxx is the core of the stack. it handles the stuff you actually do every day when running cold email campaigns.

**what it does:**
- draft and store email templates
- build multi-step follow-up sequences
- manage contact lists
- track which emails you've sent
- organize campaigns by client or niche

**what it doesn't do:**
- automated sending (you still send from your email client)
- inbox warmup (use a free warmup tool separately)
- inbox rotation across 30+ accounts (you need Instantly or Smartlead for that)

the tradeoff is clear. if you're sending 500-5,000 emails per month from 1-3 accounts, ColdMaxx handles the workflow. if you're an agency sending 50,000 emails per month across dozens of inboxes, you need paid infrastructure.

but here's the thing most people don't admit: 90% of solopreneurs running cold email are in the first category. they don't need 30 rotating inboxes. they need 1-3 accounts, good copy, and a system to track follow-ups.

that's ColdMaxx.

**try it:** [coldmaxx-app.surge.sh](https://coldmaxx-app.surge.sh)

## Cold Email ROI Calculator: know your numbers

the single biggest mistake in cold outreach is not tracking ROI. you send 1,000 emails, get 30 replies, book 5 calls, close 1 deal. is that good? depends on what the deal is worth and what you spent to get it.

the calculator takes your inputs:

- emails sent per month
- reply rate (industry average: 3-8%)
- meeting booking rate (from replies)
- close rate (from meetings)
- average deal value
- monthly tool costs

and tells you:

- cost per reply
- cost per meeting
- cost per close
- total ROI
- whether the campaign is profitable

it takes 30 seconds to fill out. most people who use it realize their cost per acquisition is higher than they assumed because they never added up all the tool subscriptions.

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/cold-email-roi-calculator](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

## Subject Line Grader: stop guessing

your subject line determines whether anyone reads your email. a 3% open rate vs an 8% open rate is the difference between a dead campaign and a profitable one.

the grader scores your subject line on:

- **length** (4-7 words is the sweet spot for cold email)
- **power words** (urgency, curiosity, relevance triggers)
- **personalization tokens** (first name, company name, role)
- **spam triggers** (words that send you straight to spam)
- **readability** (grade level, complexity)

it gives you a score out of 100 and specific suggestions. "add the recipient's first name." "remove the word 'free' from the subject line." "shorten from 12 words to 6."

**try it:** [printmaxx-tools.surge.sh/subject-line-grader](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh)

## the comparison research

before i built ColdMaxx, i spent 20+ hours comparing every cold email tool on the market. i turned that research into comparison pages so you don't have to.

**Instantly vs Lemlist:** Instantly wins on volume ($30/mo, unlimited accounts). Lemlist wins on personalization ($59/mo, custom images in emails). if you're doing high-volume outreach, Instantly. if you're doing highly personalized 1-to-1 outreach, Lemlist. if you're on a budget, ColdMaxx.

[instantly-vs-lemlist.surge.sh](https://instantly-vs-lemlist.surge.sh)

**ColdMaxx vs Instantly:** the full breakdown of $0/mo vs $30/mo. feature by feature. what you gain, what you give up.

[coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh](https://coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh)

**7 Best Cold Email Tools ranked:** all 7 major tools tested and ranked by deliverability, price, and send limits.

[best-cold-email-tools.surge.sh](https://best-cold-email-tools.surge.sh)

## how this stack works together

here's my actual daily workflow:

**morning (15 min):**
1. open ColdMaxx, check which follow-ups are due today
2. personalize templates with prospect-specific first lines
3. send from Gmail (yes, manually. 30-50 emails takes 15 minutes.)

**weekly (30 min):**
1. pull numbers into the ROI Calculator. check cost per reply and close rate trends.
2. test 3-5 new subject lines through the Subject Line Grader
3. A/B test the top 2 in next week's campaigns

**monthly (1 hour):**
1. review ROI by campaign. kill anything below 2% reply rate after 3 optimizations.
2. check the comparison pages for updated competitor pricing (they change quarterly).
3. adjust templates based on what's converting.

total time: about 4 hours/month managing cold email. total cost: $0.

## the honest tradeoff

i'm not going to pretend this replaces everything. here's what the paid tools do better:

**Instantly ($30/mo):** automated warmup is real. if you're scaling past 5,000 emails per month, their warmup pool of 10,000+ accounts keeps you out of spam better than anything free. their inbox rotation across unlimited accounts is also genuinely useful at scale.

**Lemlist ($59/mo):** custom image personalization (your prospect's LinkedIn photo embedded in the email) is a real conversion booster. ColdMaxx doesn't do this.

**Smartlead ($39/mo):** their master inbox that aggregates replies from all accounts into one view saves real time if you're managing 20+ sending accounts.

but those are scale problems. and most people reading this aren't at scale yet. they're at "trying to land my first 5 clients" stage. and at that stage, paying $100+/month for tools before you've made a dollar from cold email is backwards.

start free. prove the channel works. then invest in paid tools when the math justifies it.

## every tool, one link

- [ColdMaxx](https://coldmaxx-app.surge.sh) (free cold email management)
- [Cold Email ROI Calculator](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh) (know your numbers)
- [Subject Line Grader](https://printmaxx-tools.surge.sh) (optimize before sending)
- [ColdMaxx vs Instantly comparison](https://coldmaxx-vs-instantly.surge.sh)
- [Instantly vs Lemlist comparison](https://instantly-vs-lemlist.surge.sh)
- [7 Best Cold Email Tools ranked](https://best-cold-email-tools.surge.sh)

$0/month. no signup. your data stays in your browser.

## PUBLISHING NOTES

### Dev.to specific (Articles 1 and 2)
- Use dev.to markdown format (triple backtick code blocks work natively)
- Set tags exactly as specified (4 tag limit on dev.to)
- Add cover image from MEDIA/generated_images/ or create via HTML-to-image pipeline
- Set canonical URL to avoid SEO conflicts if cross-posting
- Enable comments (engagement signal for algorithm)
- Post between 7-9 AM EST Tuesday-Thursday for maximum visibility

### Medium specific (Articles 3 and 4)
- Submit to relevant publications: "Better Programming," "The Startup," "Towards Data Science" (article 3), "The Startup," "Better Marketing" (article 4)
- Medium allows 5 tags. add the listed tags plus "Technology" or "Entrepreneurship" as the 5th
- Set subtitle as specified
- Add bio at bottom: "i build free tools and ship fast. follow @printmaxxer for the build log."
- Medium's algorithm favors articles over 7 min read time. all 4 articles clear this.
- Best posting times: Tuesday-Thursday 8-11 AM EST

### Cross-posting strategy
- Publish on the primary platform FIRST (dev.to or medium as marked)
- Wait 48 hours for indexing
- Cross-post to the other platform with canonical URL pointing to the original
- Also cross-post to Hashnode (dev.to articles especially perform well there)
- Share links on Twitter/X with a hook quote from the article (not just "new article")

### Human actions required
- [ ] Create dev.to account if not exists (2 min)
- [ ] Create Medium account if not exists (2 min)
- [ ] Publish Article 1 on dev.to (5 min)
- [ ] Publish Article 2 on dev.to (5 min)
- [ ] Publish Article 3 on Medium (5 min)
- [ ] Publish Article 4 on Medium (5 min)
- [ ] Share all 4 on Twitter/X with hook quotes (5 min)
- [ ] Submit Medium articles to publications (10 min)
